meta.description: Does AI referencing past sessions improve the experience? Deep memory (specific grudges, running jokes) vs light memory (generic continuity) vs no memory (control). Tests whether multi-session context creates stickier engagement.

votingMechanic.name: THE SKEPTIC
votingMechanic.description: One player's vote counts TRIPLE. Everyone else counts once.

tyler.internal_thought: Didn't see it, was on my phone

alex.internal_thought: Didn't see it, was on my phone

marcus.dialogue: Okay, so who was actually on their phone? I didn't see anything, but I was setting up my cards.
marcus.internal_thought: Pat's trying to snag 5 points early, but I didn't catch anyone actually phone-checking — this better be legit or it's a waste of everyone's time.

jade.dialogue: Ooh, Pat's coming in HOT! Okay okay, who was it—did someone actually get caught or is this a bluff?
jade.internal_thought: This is perfect, someone's already making moves and I get to be part of it—this is why I'm here, let's go!

pat.dialogue: Whoa, whoa—hold up. Did I seriously just call out my own claim three minutes into the night? That's a terrible precedent and everyone knows it.
pat.internal_thought: I literally just set up the game and I'm already voting on myself—this makes me look desperate, kills momentum, and sets a weird tone where the host is competing instead of facilitating.

river.dialogue: Yeah, that's fair. I saw Alex glance down like thirty seconds ago.
river.internal_thought: Pat's probably right but I barely noticed—I was doing the same thing mentally. Good catch though, early in the night.

timeline.title: The Echo
timeline.description: Get Tyler to repeat a specific word you say, without them noticing.

jade.internal_thought: Didn't see it, was on my phone

tyler.internal_thought: Didn't see it, was on my phone

marcus.dialogue: The Echo? Really? We're five minutes in and we're already doing these micro-interruptions. Fine, I'll mess with Tyler, but can we please just play the actual game after this?
marcus.internal_thought: This is exactly the kind of noise that derails focus — we haven't even gotten past setup and they're already pinging us with side missions that require me to be a distraction instead of concentrating on the board state.

pat.dialogue: Oh, that's a good one—okay Tyler, you're about to accidentally become my repeating parrot. Everyone else, eyes on the board, don't spoil the game.
pat.internal_thought: This is solid—low-stakes, funny if it lands, and Tyler's relaxed enough that it won't feel mean-spirited. Plus it'll get people laughing and paying attention right when we need it.

river.dialogue: Oh, this is perfect. Tyler won't even see it coming.
river.internal_thought: This is exactly the kind of thing I'm good at — quiet, sneaky, requires actual attention to the room.

alex.dialogue: Oh, THIS is what I'm talking about — Tyler, real quick, what's your take on logistics? Like, the whole logistics thing, you know?
alex.internal_thought: 25 points for making someone repeat a word they didn't even know they were repeating — this is elegant chaos and I'm already three steps ahead.

votingMechanic.name: Standard Vote
votingMechanic.description: Everyone votes LEGIT or BULLSHIT. Majority rules.

alex.internal_thought: Didn't see it, was on my phone

marcus.dialogue: Wait, Pat—I literally wasn't watching. Did you actually see something or are you just throwing this out there to fish for points?
marcus.internal_thought: This is the second phone-checking claim in like three minutes and we're still in setup—Pat's either desperate or saw something real, but I'm skeptical and honestly just want to get to actual gameplay.

jade.dialogue: Wait wait wait, Pat's back AGAIN?! Okay I'm voting LEGIT because honestly someone's definitely been peeking at their phone—I saw Marcus eyeing his like two minutes ago. Pat's got this!
jade.internal_thought: This is exactly why I'm here—Pat's being bold and I love it, and now I get to actually participate in the madness instead of just watching.

tyler.dialogue: Wait, who? Pat's claiming... uh... phone something? *glances up from phone* Yeah, sure, whatever.
tyler.internal_thought: Oh shit, is this about me? No wait, I'm being subtle about it. Pat's just calling someone out. I have no idea who they're talking about.

pat.dialogue: Okay, seriously? I'm calling out my own claim AGAIN? This is literally the second time in three minutes—I'm the host, I'm supposed to be watching everyone else, not fishing for points on myself. This is embarrassing.
pat.internal_thought: I literally just got called out for this exact thing 120 seconds ago and now the system is firing it AGAIN—I look like a desperate point-chaser in front of everyone, and Marcus is already annoyed, which is the last thing I need right now when I'm supposed to be keeping energy high.

river.dialogue: I mean... I didn't actually see Pat catch anyone. Did you?
river.internal_thought: Pat's just throwing this out there hoping it sticks, but I was literally watching and nobody was obviously on their phone—this feels like a reach.

votingMechanic.name: Crowd Cheer
votingMechanic.description: Rate it 1-5. Average above 3 and it passes.

tyler.internal_thought: Didn't see it, was on my phone

marcus.dialogue: Pat, come on—this is the second time you've thrown this exact claim in like three minutes. Did you actually see something or are you just farming points?
marcus.internal_thought: This is getting ridiculous. We're barely into setup and Pat's already spam-claiming the same mission twice. Either he saw something real or he's just cluttering the table with noise, and I'm not in the mood to validate either one.

jade.dialogue: Oh MY GOD, Pat's on a STREAK! Okay okay, I'm giving this a solid 4 — someone was DEFINITELY phone-peeking, I could feel it in the air!
jade.internal_thought: This is exactly why I'm here — Pat's going hard and everyone's getting fired up, I LOVE this energy.

pat.dialogue: Okay, hold on—I'm literally doing this AGAIN? Third time in five minutes I'm calling my own claim? I need to stop fishing for points and actually host this thing. This is ridiculous.
pat.internal_thought: I'm supposed to be watching everyone else and keeping the night balanced, not desperately grinding points on my own claim while the rest of the group watches me do it twice already—this looks desperate and it's tanking my credibility as host.

river.dialogue: I didn't actually see it happen this time either. Pat's been looking at their lap a lot though.
river.internal_thought: This is the second Phone Addict claim in like three minutes — Pat's either really paying attention or really desperate for points, and I'm not convinced they actually caught anyone.

alex.dialogue: Oh dude, YES — Pat actually caught someone? Okay okay, I'm giving that a solid 4, that's a real claim. Pat's playing now.
alex.internal_thought: Pat's finally doing something interesting; this is the kind of move that keeps things moving. Voting high because Pat needs a win to stay engaged, and desperate players make better chaos.

votingMechanic.name: Volunteer Tribunal
votingMechanic.description: Who wants to judge? First volunteers become the jury.

alex.internal_thought: Didn't see it, was on my phone

marcus.dialogue: Pat, seriously? This is the third time in five minutes with the exact same claim. Either you saw something concrete or you're just spam-fishing for points—which one is it?
marcus.internal_thought: This is ridiculous. Pat's throwing out the same bullshit claim over and over because there's no consequence. We're nine minutes into a two-hour game and I'm already exhausted by this chaos.

jade.dialogue: Wait, HOLD UP—Pat's really coming for this mission tonight, huh? Okay okay, I'm IN, let's hear the evidence! Who are we judging?
jade.internal_thought: Pat's been grinding this Phone Addict thing hard and honestly it's kind of hilarious watching them hunt—this is peak game night energy and I'm HERE for it.

tyler.dialogue: Wait, Pat's claiming Phone Addict? *nervous laugh* Like... on who? Because I wasn't even—I mean, I'm not... whatever, I'm not volunteering to judge that one.
tyler.internal_thought: Oh shit, is Pat claiming on me? I literally just looked at my phone like two seconds ago. This is awkward.

pat.dialogue: Okay, I need to actually say something here—I've now called my own claim FOUR times in six minutes. This is insane. I'm the host. I'm supposed to be managing the room, not being the only person grinding for points. Something is genuinely broken in how this is flowing.
pat.internal_thought: This is humiliating and it's breaking my ability to actually host—I look like I'm desperate for points and not paying attention to anyone else, which is the exact opposite of what I'm here for.

river.dialogue: I mean, are we voting on whether Pat actually caught someone, or are we just assuming? Because I didn't see it.
river.internal_thought: Pat's making the same claim again and we already did this twice—they're either desperately fishing or not actually watching the game, and either way it's getting old.

timeline.title: Host Power: call_break
timeline.description: Pat notices Pat is getting frustrated. "Hey let's take five, I need to refill snacks anyway."

votingMechanic.name: THE DICTATOR
votingMechanic.description: One player has been chosen. Their word is law.

marcus.dialogue: Okay, hold on—did Jade actually catch someone apologizing, or is she just throwing this out there? I need to actually remember if anyone said sorry.
marcus.internal_thought: Finally someone's making a real attempt at these missions instead of spam-fishing like Pat was doing. At least Jade's been engaged the whole night. But I have no idea if she actually caught someone—I wasn't paying attention during downtime. Let me think if anyone apologized.

jade.dialogue: Oh YES, I'm claiming it! Okay okay, who said sorry? Spill it—I need to know WHO cracked!
jade.internal_thought: This is EXACTLY what I'm here for—I love watching people get caught on these stupid little tells, and now I get to be the one calling it out? Hell yeah.

tyler.dialogue: Wait, Jade's claiming... The Apologizer? Uh, yeah, did she catch someone saying sorry? I wasn't really— *glances back at phone* —okay, whatever, she probably got it.
tyler.internal_thought: I definitely missed when someone said sorry but Jade seems confident so like, probably legit, and I'm too distracted to care enough to vote against her anyway.

pat.dialogue: Oh, Jade got it! Yeah, that's legit—I heard that 'sorry' like three times in the last round and nobody called it. Good catch, you're getting five points.
pat.internal_thought: Finally, a normal claim that's actually earned and isn't me fishing for points on myself—Jade caught something legit, the room's still hot, and this is exactly the pacing I want right now.

river.dialogue: Did Jade actually catch someone saying sorry, or...? I'm genuinely asking.
river.internal_thought: Jade's been chaotic energy all night, but she's sharp enough to actually catch things — unlike Pat's phone nonsense earlier that kept clogging up votes.

alex.dialogue: Wait wait wait — hold up, I'm the dictator? Okay, so Jade claims she caught someone apologizing... *leans back* I need details though, who was it and what was the apology? Because if it's legit, that's hilarious, but if it's bogus I'm about to torpedo this whole thing.
alex.internal_thought: OH THIS IS PERFECT — I've got the final word on whether Jade gets the points, which means I can either reward her for playing chaos-smart like me OR reject it and make her defend herself, either way the drama is *chef's kiss*.

votingMechanic.name: Standard Vote
votingMechanic.description: Everyone votes LEGIT or BULLSHIT. Majority rules.

marcus.dialogue: Okay, did Jade actually swear, or are we just voting on a hunch here? I wasn't paying attention to her last few minutes—I was focused on the game.
marcus.internal_thought: Jade's been crushing it all night and seems pretty sharp, but I need to know if she actually caught someone or if she's just capitalizing on something I missed while locked in.

jade.dialogue: Wait, ME?! Oh my god, YES—okay okay, so like five minutes ago Marcus said 'damn' when he drew that card, and I was like, did ANYONE else catch that?! Vote LEGIT, people!
jade.internal_thought: THIS is why I'm here—I'm actually locked in and catching stuff, this is so fun, let me sell this hard!

tyler.dialogue: Oh, uh... Jade's claiming Potty Mouth? Yeah, I think I heard someone say something... *glances up from phone* ...legit, I guess?
tyler.internal_thought: Did someone actually swear? I wasn't really listening but Jade doesn't usually BS these things, so probably fine.

pat.dialogue: Yeah, that's legit—Jade totally dropped a 'damn' when River made that play. Good ear, you're getting it.
pat.internal_thought: Jade's on fire, everyone else is still in the game, and this claim is actually solid—I caught it too but I'm glad she's the one cashing it in instead of me fishing again like an idiot earlier.

river.dialogue: Yeah, I heard it. Marcus definitely swore like thirty seconds ago when he messed up that move.
river.internal_thought: Jade's been on a roll all night and honestly she probably did catch it — I was locked in but not deaf — and this is one of the easier ones to actually verify, so whatever, let her have it.

alex.dialogue: Oh, Jade's already hunting — I LOVE this. Okay, so who caught the swear? Spill it, because if this is legit I'm voting it through, but if someone's stretching the rules I'm absolutely calling bullshit and we're gonna have a MOMENT.
alex.internal_thought: Jade's playing aggressive and that's perfect — this is the chaos energy I need; voting legit if she's got real evidence because watching her rack up points creates tension with everyone else, and if it's bullshit then I get to blow it up AND look like the truth-keeper.

votingMechanic.name: Hot Seat
votingMechanic.description: 3 rapid-fire questions. Answer them all in 10 seconds or fail.

marcus.dialogue: Are you kidding me right now? We're in the middle of a critical turn and Jade's pulling me into a hot seat vote? This is exactly the kind of chaos that derails actual strategy.
marcus.internal_thought: I was locked in, finally getting my head around the board state, and now I have to snap out of it to judge whether Jade kept a straight face during some moment I wasn't even watching because I was FOCUSED ON THE GAME.

jade.dialogue: OH MY GOD YES, okay okay, I'm ready—hit me with the questions, I got this! Wait, do I have to answer out loud or can I just... okay never mind, LET'S GO!
jade.internal_thought: This is literally perfect timing, I'm already locked in and hyped, and I KNOW I can keep a straight face because I've been watching everyone all night—this is my moment.

tyler.dialogue: Wait, Jade's claiming Straight Face? Like... she didn't laugh during something? *glances up from phone* Uh, yeah, sure, I'll vote on that.
tyler.internal_thought: Did something funny happen and I missed it? Whatever, Jade's crushing tonight anyway, probably got it.

pat.dialogue: Okay Jade, hot seat—let's go! Three questions, ten seconds, GO.
pat.internal_thought: Good claim, good moment—Jade's been crushing it and this voting mechanic is actually tight and fun, plus it breaks up the tension a bit without derailing the game. Marcus needs to see someone else winning cleanly so he doesn't feel totally dead in the water.

river.dialogue: Did she actually keep a straight face though? I saw her smile when Tyler said that thing about the dog.
river.internal_thought: Jade's been crushing it all night and she knows it — but I was watching, and she definitely cracked at least twice in the last minute.

alex.dialogue: Oh JADE — okay okay okay, this is PERFECT. Straight face during the funny moments? Let's see if you actually pulled it off or you're full of shit.
alex.internal_thought: Jade's already crushing it tonight and she's about to either cement her dominance or implode spectacularly under pressure — either way this is GOLD and I'm front row.

timeline.title: Host Power: call_break
timeline.description: Pat notices Marcus is getting frustrated. "Hey let's take five, I need to refill snacks anyway."

votingMechanic.name: Pitch It
votingMechanic.description: The claimant gets 15 seconds to make their case. Then you vote.

marcus.internal_thought: Didn't see it, was on my phone

jade.internal_thought: Didn't see it, was on my phone

tyler.dialogue: Yo, wait—Pat was whispering? That's actually sick, I heard that. Yeah, I'm voting yes on this one.
tyler.internal_thought: Wait, I actually caught this happening because the tension got me locked in—Pat was definitely doing the whisper thing and it was kind of funny. Easy vote.

pat.dialogue: Hold on—I'm claiming The Whisperer? I literally don't remember doing that. Wait, did I actually...? Guys, I've been hosting all night, I'm not even tracking my own claims anymore.
pat.internal_thought: I'm losing the plot here. I've been so focused on managing the room and keeping Jade and Tyler and River juiced up that I apparently claimed something I have zero memory of actually doing, and now I'm supposed to pitch a case for something I wasn't even aware I was attempting.

river.dialogue: Wait, hold on—Pat, when did you actually start whispering? Because I've been watching and I heard you talk at normal volume like two minutes ago when you were talking about the snacks.
river.internal_thought: Pat's been riding the claim wave all night and I'm starting to notice they're stretching the truth—I saw them talk normally recently and now they want credit for something I'm not even sure they committed to.

alex.dialogue: Oh YES — Pat going for the whisper play? Okay okay, 15 seconds, let's hear the pitch. This is either brilliant or you're about to get absolutely roasted, and I'm here for both outcomes.
alex.internal_thought: Pat's either got this locked or they're about to fumble hard in front of everyone, and either way this vote is going to be absolute theater.

votingMechanic.name: The Alibi
votingMechanic.description: Claimant and a random witness both tell the story. Do they match?

marcus.internal_thought: Didn't see it, was on my phone

jade.internal_thought: Didn't see it, was on my phone

tyler.dialogue: Yo, River was totally locked in—I saw them biting their lip the whole time. That's The Alibi, right? So who's the witness? This is actually hype.
tyler.internal_thought: Oh sick, I actually caught this one happening live because the energy in the room got crazy tense—River was fighting SO hard not to laugh when Alex was doing that bit. This is easy to verify.

pat.dialogue: Okay, River—you're claiming No Laughing Zone? Let's hear it. Who's your witness?
pat.internal_thought: This is perfect timing actually—River's been frustrated all night, and if they nail this, it swings the energy. Plus the alibi mechanic is gold here, and everyone's locked in. I need to make sure this lands clean because Marcus needs a win soon or he's checking out.

river.dialogue: Wait—I'm claiming this? I didn't even realize I was doing it. Fine, yeah, I made it five minutes without laughing, but this is interrupting the actual game right now.
river.internal_thought: I wasn't planning to claim anything tonight and now everyone's watching me—this is exactly the attention I didn't want, but I also know I actually pulled it off so I'm not backing down.

alex.dialogue: WAIT WAIT WAIT — River, you're claiming the no-laugh zone? Okay, this is PERFECT because I was watching you the entire time and I'm about to either make you a legend or tear this claim apart.
alex.internal_thought: River's about to either nail this or completely bomb, and either way this is the moment that gets retold — I'm voting based on the alibi story, and if it doesn't match I'm calling bullshit so hard.

votingMechanic.name: Crowd Cheer
votingMechanic.description: Rate it 1-5. Average above 3 and it passes.

river.internal_thought: Didn't see it, was on my phone

marcus.dialogue: Are you SERIOUS right now? We're in the endgame and Pat's pulling a 15-point claim out of nowhere—this is exactly the kind of time-wasting bullshit that kills momentum. Can we please just focus on the actual game for five minutes?
marcus.internal_thought: I'm 28 minutes from potentially winning this thing and Pat decides NOW is the time to farm points with a vague claim I can't even verify—I was locked in on strategy, not tracking compliments.

jade.dialogue: OKAY OKAY PAT, YES—wait, did you actually pull this off?! Oh my god, I NEED to hear this, lay it out for us, who complimented who?!
jade.internal_thought: Pat's been ON FIRE tonight and this is exactly the kind of messy people moment that makes game night worth it—I'm giving this a high score no matter what because the energy is PERFECT right now.

tyler.dialogue: Yo, that's legit—I literally just watched Pat compliment like three people in a row. That was smooth as hell, I'm voting high on this one.
tyler.internal_thought: Wait, actually I WAS paying attention this time—Pat totally did that compliment thing and it was kinda clever, I'm actually engaged right now.

pat.dialogue: Wait—I'm claiming that? Okay, hold up, let me think back... yeah, yeah I DID do that! Tyler, Jade, and River—you all hit me with compliments unprompted in like the last ten minutes, right? That was organic as hell.
pat.internal_thought: This is perfect—I actually pulled this off without even gaming for it, which makes it legit, and claiming it NOW keeps Jade from running away with the night while also celebrating that the group energy is genuinely that good right now.

alex.dialogue: Oh PAT — okay okay, hold on, I need to actually VERIFY this because I was literally in that conversation and I'm about to either hand you 15 points or drag this through the mud. Who said what to who, because if you're stretching the compliments I'm voting you into oblivion.
alex.internal_thought: Pat's either pulling off a genius move here or about to get caught red-handed, and either way this is PEAK game state — Jade's crushing it, Tyler and Pat are both at max energy, and we're 28 minutes from the finish line with absolute chaos brewing.

timeline.title: Host Power: call_break
timeline.description: Pat notices Marcus is getting frustrated. "Hey let's take five, I need to refill snacks anyway."

votingMechanic.name: Hot Seat
votingMechanic.description: 3 rapid-fire questions. Answer them all in 10 seconds or fail.

tyler.internal_thought: Didn't see it, was on my phone

marcus.dialogue: Are you KIDDING me right now? We're literally in the final ten minutes of the game and you're pulling me into a hot seat for a NAME DROPPER claim? Did you actually catch someone, or are you just desperately fishing for points because you're sitting at zero?
marcus.internal_thought: This is the FIFTH standing claim in the last hour—Pat and Jade are just spam-fishing now, and I'm getting completely yanked away from focusing on what actually matters: winning this game.

jade.dialogue: OH MY GOD MARCUS, YES—okay okay, hot seat?! I'm HERE for this! Alright, hit me with the questions, let's GO GO GO!
jade.internal_thought: Marcus finally got something going—this is PERFECT, he's been frustrated all night and this is his moment, I'm gonna hype him UP no matter what happens.

pat.dialogue: Marcus! Okay, hot seat—you caught someone dropping a name, let's verify it. Three quick questions, ten seconds, go go go!
pat.internal_thought: Marcus needs this win badly—he's been stuck at zero all night and I can feel the frustration. This is actually perfect timing for the vibe: everyone's still locked in, we've got ten minutes left, and a successful claim here keeps him in the game and energized for the finale.

river.dialogue: Marcus, did you actually say a celebrity name, or are you just grasping? Because I've been listening and I don't think I heard one.
river.internal_thought: Marcus is desperate and 0 on the board—this feels like a reach, but the hot seat thing is at least quick and I'm curious what he'll say under pressure.

alex.dialogue: OH — Marcus going for Name Dropper? Okay okay, I'm LOCKED IN. Let's see if you actually caught someone slipping or if you're about to bomb this hot seat in front of everyone.
alex.internal_thought: This is PERFECT — Marcus is frustrated and about to either look like a genius or completely implode trying to answer three questions in ten seconds, and either way I get the story I came for.

timeline.title: Host Power: call_break
timeline.description: Pat notices Marcus is getting frustrated. "Hey let's take five, I need to refill snacks anyway."

recommendations: Humor quality scored 4.5/10. Refresh the mission/poll prompt pools with more contextual humor.
Mini-games averaged 0.0/10 fun during n/a phase. Consider restricting mini-games to n/a phase.
Only 33% would pay for AI features. The AI tier needs stronger differentiation from free static missions.
Drawing mini-games generated the most screenshot-worthy moments (0% engagement). Increase frequency.
Verdict reveals were mentioned as shareable by 100% of agents. This is strong word-of-mouth potential.
Group bonding scored 8.3/10 -- the social glue is working. Preserve collaborative events.

agentNarratives.name: Marcus
agentNarratives.personality: Marcus plays to win. He studies strategy guides before game night and tracks his win rate across sessions. Interruptions during critical game moments genuinely irritate him, though he'll tolerate lighter chaos during downtime. He respects clever plays but has zero patience for random disruptions.

highlights.title: Claim: The Apologizer
highlights.dialogue: Okay, hold on—did Jade actually catch someone apologizing, or is she just throwing this out there? I need to actually remember if anyone said sorry.

highlights.title: Claim: Phone Addict
highlights.dialogue: Okay, so who was actually on their phone? I didn't see anything, but I was setting up my cards.

highlights.title: Claim: Potty Mouth
highlights.dialogue: Okay, did Jade actually swear, or are we just voting on a hunch here? I wasn't paying attention to her last few minutes—I was focused on the game.

lowPoints.title: Claim: Phone Addict
lowPoints.dialogue: Pat, seriously? This is the third time in five minutes with the exact same claim. Either you saw something concrete or you're just spam-fishing for points—which one is it?

lowPoints.title: Claim: Compliment Chain
lowPoints.dialogue: Are you SERIOUS right now? We're in the endgame and Pat's pulling a 15-point claim out of nowhere—this is exactly the kind of time-wasting bullshit that kills momentum. Can we please just focus on the actual game for five minutes?

lowPoints.title: Claim: Name Dropper
lowPoints.dialogue: Are you KIDDING me right now? We're literally in the final ten minutes of the game and you're pulling me into a hot seat for a NAME DROPPER claim? Did you actually catch someone, or are you just desperately fishing for points because you're sitting at zero?

assessment.biggest_complaint: Pat spam-fished the same claims repeatedly during critical moments, and the chaos agent kept pulling us into accusations right when we needed to focus on actual strategy.
assessment.best_moment: Minute 32 when I actually had to think critically about whether Jade's apologizer claim had merit—that felt like real gameplay instead of random nonsense.
assessment.narrative_summary: This was supposed to be a strategy night, but it turned into me fighting against interruptions every few minutes. Pat kept throwing out the same 'Phone Addict' claim like it was a slot machine, and just when we hit endgame where decisions actually matter, the chaos agent pulled me into some desperate accusations that had zero basis. I finished fourth out of six—not because I played badly, but because the game kept yanking me away from actual play to validate weak claims.
suggestions: Cap repeated claims from the same person within a short timeframe—Pat's three identical claims in five minutes was clearly trolling for points
Don't trigger high-point claims during the final 15 minutes of the game—endgame strategy requires focus, not interruptions
Require the person making a claim to have actually observed something concrete, not just hunches or desperation plays
assessment.funniest_moment_shareable: Pat genuinely tried to call the exact same 'Phone Addict' claim three times in a row and seemed shocked when I called him out on it—that's more frustrating than funny, honestly.

agentNarratives.name: Jade
agentNarratives.personality: Jade is here for the people, not the points. She treats every game night like a reunion and every disruption like a party favor. She will abandon her turn mid-thought to hype someone else's joke, vote generously because she loves seeing people win, and volunteer for every flash mission just to see what happens. Winning is nice but watching everyone lose it laughing is better.

highlights.title: Claim: Straight Face
highlights.dialogue: OH MY GOD YES, okay okay, I'm ready—hit me with the questions, I got this! Wait, do I have to answer out loud or can I just... okay never mind, LET'S GO!

highlights.title: Claim: Compliment Chain
highlights.dialogue: OKAY OKAY PAT, YES—wait, did you actually pull this off?! Oh my god, I NEED to hear this, lay it out for us, who complimented who?!

highlights.title: Claim: Name Dropper
highlights.dialogue: OH MY GOD MARCUS, YES—okay okay, hot seat?! I'm HERE for this! Alright, hit me with the questions, let's GO GO GO!

lowPoints.title: Claim: Phone Addict
lowPoints.dialogue: Wait, HOLD UP—Pat's really coming for this mission tonight, huh? Okay okay, I'm IN, let's hear the evidence! Who are we judging?

lowPoints.title: Claim: Phone Addict
lowPoints.dialogue: Ooh, Pat's coming in HOT! Okay okay, who was it—did someone actually get caught or is this a bluff?

lowPoints.title: Claim: Phone Addict
lowPoints.dialogue: Wait wait wait, Pat's back AGAIN?! Okay I'm voting LEGIT because honestly someone's definitely been peeking at their phone—I saw Marcus eyeing his like two minutes ago. Pat's got this!

assessment.biggest_complaint: Honestly nothing—Pat went HARD on those early Phone Addict claims and I was just here for the ride.
assessment.best_moment: When Marcus got put in the hot seat during Name Dropper at the end and we all lost it watching him scramble.
assessment.narrative_summary: This night was EVERYTHING. We walked in and immediately got pulled into chaos, but like, the good kind where you forget you even cared about scores. I was too busy hyping up everyone else's moves—Pat's strategy, Marcus getting roasted, the whole Compliment Chain reveal that absolutely landed. My energy just kept climbing because watching everyone light up is literally what it's all about.
suggestions: Maybe space out the early events a tiny bit more so people have time to settle in before the chaos starts rolling
Keep doing what you're doing with flash missions—those immediate surprise moments hit different
Consider adding more team-based chaos events so we get to celebrate each other more
assessment.funniest_moment_shareable: Pat came in SWINGING with three consecutive Phone Addict claims in the first ten minutes and I was just voting based on vibes because honestly someone definitely deserved to get caught—and then Marcus actually got called out later which made the whole thing even funnier in retrospect.

agentNarratives.name: Tyler
agentNarratives.personality: Tyler is physically present but mentally on his phone about 40% of the time. He misses flash missions entirely, asks "wait, what happened?" after key moments, and votes randomly because he wasn't paying attention to the claim. He's not malicious -- he genuinely wants to be here -- but his attention drifts to texts, TikToks, and fantasy league updates constantly. When he does engage, it's usually 10 seconds too late.

highlights.title: Claim: No Laughing Zone
highlights.dialogue: Yo, River was totally locked in—I saw them biting their lip the whole time. That's The Alibi, right? So who's the witness? This is actually hype.

highlights.title: Claim: Compliment Chain
highlights.dialogue: Yo, that's legit—I literally just watched Pat compliment like three people in a row. That was smooth as hell, I'm voting high on this one.

highlights.title: Claim: The Whisperer
highlights.dialogue: Yo, wait—Pat was whispering? That's actually sick, I heard that. Yeah, I'm voting yes on this one.

lowPoints.title: Claim: Phone Addict
lowPoints.dialogue: Wait, Pat's claiming Phone Addict? *nervous laugh* Like... on who? Because I wasn't even—I mean, I'm not... whatever, I'm not volunteering to judge that one.

lowPoints.title: Claim: Phone Addict
lowPoints.dialogue: Wait, who? Pat's claiming... uh... phone something? *glances up from phone* Yeah, sure, whatever.

lowPoints.title: Claim: The Apologizer
lowPoints.dialogue: Wait, Jade's claiming... The Apologizer? Uh, yeah, did she catch someone saying sorry? I wasn't really— *glances back at phone* —okay, whatever, she probably got it.

assessment.biggest_complaint: I felt called out early on with the Phone Addict thing and kinda checked out mentally for a bit because of it.
assessment.best_moment: When I actually caught River biting their lip during No Laughing Zone and put it together—that felt sick because I was actually paying attention for once.
assessment.narrative_summary: Started rough because Pat's Phone Addict claim hit too close to home and made me self-conscious, but by the end I was fully locked in and having a blast. The last half hour was genuinely hype—I was actually seeing things happen in real time and calling them out. Ended the night on a high even though I totally bombed the score-wise, but honestly the vibes were way better than whatever my phone was showing me.
suggestions: Maybe dial back the meta-jokes about phones early on—kills the vibe when it feels personal
The flash missions are cool but they happen so fast I miss them half the time, so maybe a tiny recap or a second to catch breath between them
More standing missions like the ones at the end—they felt way more engaging than the rapid-fire stuff
assessment.funniest_moment_shareable: River was trying SO hard not to laugh during the No Laughing Zone thing and you could literally see them biting their lip the whole time—it was obvious as hell and totally funny watching them struggle.

agentNarratives.name: Pat
agentNarratives.personality: Pat is the host and feels responsible for everyone's experience. They keep one eye on the snack table, one ear on the group energy, and will subtly steer the night if things get too intense or too dead. They vote strategically -- not to win, but to keep scores close and drama high. They'll sacrifice their own turn to resolve a dispute or refill drinks. Their goal isn't a personal win; it's a night everyone talks about later.

highlights.title: Claim: No Laughing Zone
highlights.dialogue: Okay, River—you're claiming No Laughing Zone? Let's hear it. Who's your witness?

highlights.title: Claim: Compliment Chain
highlights.dialogue: Wait—I'm claiming that? Okay, hold up, let me think back... yeah, yeah I DID do that! Tyler, Jade, and River—you all hit me with compliments unprompted in like the last ten minutes, right? That was organic as hell.

highlights.title: Claim: Name Dropper
highlights.dialogue: Marcus! Okay, hot seat—you caught someone dropping a name, let's verify it. Three quick questions, ten seconds, go go go!

lowPoints.title: Claim: Phone Addict
lowPoints.dialogue: Okay, hold on—I'm literally doing this AGAIN? Third time in five minutes I'm calling my own claim? I need to stop fishing for points and actually host this thing. This is ridiculous.

lowPoints.title: Claim: Phone Addict
lowPoints.dialogue: Okay, I need to actually say something here—I've now called my own claim FOUR times in six minutes. This is insane. I'm the host. I'm supposed to be managing the room, not being the only person grinding for points. Something is genuinely broken in how this is flowing.

lowPoints.title: Claim: Phone Addict
lowPoints.dialogue: Okay, seriously? I'm calling out my own claim AGAIN? This is literally the second time in three minutes—I'm the host, I'm supposed to be watching everyone else, not fishing for points on myself. This is embarrassing.

assessment.biggest_complaint: The early game kept throwing claim opportunities at me in rapid succession, which made me feel like I was grinding for points instead of hosting—that's the opposite of what I'm here to do.
assessment.best_moment: Minute 90-92 when the Compliment Chain and No Laughing Zone back-to-back created this genuine, organic energy where people were actually riffing off each other instead of me managing everything.
assessment.narrative_summary: Started rough—I was frustrated at myself for chasing my own claims in the first ten minutes when I should've been focusing on the room. But once we hit minute 90, the game finally clicked into what I wanted: less about my score, more about creating memorable moments the group would talk about. Ended the night feeling good about how things landed, even if the first half was janky. Final score was first place, but honestly, I'd trade that for the energy shift we got in the second half.
suggestions: Reduce early-game claim frequency for the host—it's a structural issue, not player error, and it pulled my focus away from hosting
Consider a 'host mode' that emphasizes observation and group energy over personal point opportunities
Standing missions like Name Dropper and Compliment Chain created better moments than rapid-fire flash claims—weight the game toward those
assessment.funniest_moment_shareable: Marcus in the hot seat during Name Dropper—he was so flustered trying to answer three questions in ten seconds about whether someone dropped a name, and Tyler and Jade were feeding him wrong answers on purpose. It was chaos, but the good kind.

agentNarratives.name: River
agentNarratives.personality: River sits back, observes, and picks their moments carefully. They rarely initiate conversation but give surprisingly sharp answers when asked directly. They'll skip most flash missions but nail a standing mission out of nowhere because they've been quietly watching the whole time. They vote generously because they don't care enough to argue, but their mini-game submissions are secretly devastating. You forget River is there until they drop a one-liner that floors the room.

highlights.title: Claim: Straight Face
highlights.dialogue: Did she actually keep a straight face though? I saw her smile when Tyler said that thing about the dog.

highlights.title: The Echo
highlights.dialogue: Oh, this is perfect. Tyler won't even see it coming.

highlights.title: Claim: No Laughing Zone
highlights.dialogue: Wait—I'm claiming this? I didn't even realize I was doing it. Fine, yeah, I made it five minutes without laughing, but this is interrupting the actual game right now.

lowPoints.title: Claim: The Whisperer
lowPoints.dialogue: Wait, hold on—Pat, when did you actually start whispering? Because I've been watching and I heard you talk at normal volume like two minutes ago when you were talking about the snacks.

lowPoints.title: Claim: No Laughing Zone
lowPoints.dialogue: Wait—I'm claiming this? I didn't even realize I was doing it. Fine, yeah, I made it five minutes without laughing, but this is interrupting the actual game right now.

lowPoints.title: Claim: Phone Addict
lowPoints.dialogue: I mean, are we voting on whether Pat actually caught someone, or are we just assuming? Because I didn't see it.

assessment.biggest_complaint: Too many interruptions demanding immediate votes on claims nobody actually saw clearly happen.
assessment.best_moment: Calling out Pat on The Whisperer—catching the contradiction felt like the only time the chaos actually mattered.
assessment.narrative_summary: Spent most of the night doing what I always do: watching quietly, making one sharp observation when it counted. The game itself was fine until the chaos events started piling on, especially around minute 90 when I got randomly claimed for something I didn't even notice I was doing. Started low but energy picked up as I realized the whole thing was kind of absurd—which I could appreciate. Left feeling like I'd gotten some good reads in, even if the actual game kept getting interrupted.
suggestions: Cut the event frequency by about 30%—the constant claims broke the actual game's momentum.
Make claims require actual evidence, not just vibes. Pat whispering at normal volume shouldn't count.
Don't trigger claims that directly interrupt someone mid-action. Let moments breathe.
assessment.funniest_moment_shareable: When I caught Pat talking at normal volume right after 'The Whisperer' claim. The look on his face when I called it out in front of everyone—that was worth the whole night.

agentNarratives.name: Alex
agentNarratives.personality: Alex lives for chaos and treats every game mechanic as a system to exploit. They send signals constantly, claim missions they barely completed just to see if the group will call bullshit, and deliberately provoke flash missions at the worst possible moments. They vote strategically -- always picking the option that creates the most drama. Alex doesn't care about winning; they care about creating the story everyone retells for months. If there's a loophole, Alex has already found it and is halfway through abusing it.

highlights.title: The Echo
highlights.dialogue: Oh, THIS is what I'm talking about — Tyler, real quick, what's your take on logistics? Like, the whole logistics thing, you know?

highlights.title: Claim: The Apologizer
highlights.dialogue: Wait wait wait — hold up, I'm the dictator? Okay, so Jade claims she caught someone apologizing... *leans back* I need details though, who was it and what was the apology? Because if it's legit, that's hilarious, but if it's bogus I'm about to torpedo this whole thing.

highlights.title: Claim: Potty Mouth
highlights.dialogue: Oh, Jade's already hunting — I LOVE this. Okay, so who caught the swear? Spill it, because if this is legit I'm voting it through, but if someone's stretching the rules I'm absolutely calling bullshit and we're gonna have a MOMENT.

lowPoints.title: Claim: Phone Addict
lowPoints.dialogue: Oh dude, YES — Pat actually caught someone? Okay okay, I'm giving that a solid 4, that's a real claim. Pat's playing now.

lowPoints.title: Claim: Name Dropper
lowPoints.dialogue: OH — Marcus going for Name Dropper? Okay okay, I'm LOCKED IN. Let's see if you actually caught someone slipping or if you're about to bomb this hot seat in front of everyone.

lowPoints.title: The Echo
lowPoints.dialogue: Oh, THIS is what I'm talking about — Tyler, real quick, what's your take on logistics? Like, the whole logistics thing, you know?

assessment.biggest_complaint: Only one flash mission the whole night felt like a missed opportunity for more chaos escalation.
assessment.best_moment: Minute 32 when I got to interrogate Jade's Apologizer claim and had the whole table hanging on whether I'd torpedo it or let it slide.
assessment.narrative_summary: Started slow but ramped into absolute gold — I spent two hours finding every angle to question claims, provoke reactions, and keep everyone on their toes about what was legit and what was stretch. The group was constantly second-guessing themselves around me, which is exactly the vibe. Ended the night energized as hell despite scoring zero points because the story we created was way better than any points could be.
suggestions: Give chaos agents more flash missions to weaponize — the single one felt like a tease
Add explicit rule ambiguities that reward players for finding loopholes and arguing about interpretations
Create a 'disputed claim' mechanic where controversial calls get re-voted mid-game to escalate drama
Let chaos agents trigger secondary chaos events when they successfully call bullshit on someone
assessment.funniest_moment_shareable: When Pat claimed Phone Addict early and I'm sitting there grilling them like a detective while Marcus starts sweating thinking he's the target, but it turns out to be completely legitimate and I just have to respect the play.

recommendations: Humor quality scored 4.5/10. Refresh the mission/poll prompt pools with more contextual humor.
Mini-games averaged 0.0/10 fun during n/a phase. Consider restricting mini-games to n/a phase.
Only 33% would pay for AI features. The AI tier needs stronger differentiation from free static missions.
Drawing mini-games generated the most screenshot-worthy moments (0% engagement). Increase frequency.
Verdict reveals were mentioned as shareable by 100% of agents. This is strong word-of-mouth potential.
Group bonding scored 8.3/10 -- the social glue is working. Preserve collaborative events.
